coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly intracellular microalgae synthesis of fluorescent CdSe QDs as a sensitive nanoprobe for determination of imatinib
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zhengwei
Chen, Jiao
Yang, Qiulian
Lan, Kai
Yan, Zhengyu
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: Currently, the bio-immobilization methods displayed great potential for the removal and recycle of highly toxic metals depending on their gentle and orderly regulated cellular environment. Herein, we have presented a facile and eco-friendly strategy for bio-immobilizing cadmium (Cd) ions and to harvest CdSe quantum dots (QDs) using microalgae cells as bio-reactors. The production process has good environmental benefits for recycling hazardous Cd metal. The biosynthesized CdSe QDs exhibited superior optical properties, good water-solubility, high crystallinity structure, and were monodispersed sphere nanoparticles with approximate range of 5–6 nm in size, clearly comfirmed by the inversed fluorescent microscope, Ultraviolet-visible, photoluminescence, transmission electron microscopy analyses and so on. Owning to their fluorescence quenching effect, the as-obtained CdSe QDs could be deemed as an effective fluorencent nanoprobe for imatinib detection. The linear range for imatinib was calculated to be from 10.43 μg mL−1 to 417.30 μg mL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.014 μg mL−1. More strikingly, the possible formation mechanism of CdSe QDs in algal cells was preliminarily speculated that could be related with the intracellular GSH, which could provide new insights for the possibility for environmental transformation of poisonous metals to fluorescent QDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microalgae cells
Biosynthesized CdSe QDs
Fluorescent probe
Imatinib
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biosynthesis of CdSe QDs by microalgae cells
Characterization of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus
Optimum conditions for CdSe QDs biosynthesis by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus
Possible mechanism of CdSe QDs formation by microalgae cells
Condition optimization of CdSe QDs-based nanoprobe
The effect of the dosage of CdSe QDs
The effect of reaction temperature and time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bright field images (top) and fluorescence images (bottom) of C. pyrenoidosa cells (A) and S. obliquus cells (B) grown in the absence of Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 (control group) and in the presence of Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 (experimental group) for 0, 4, 8, 12, and 16 days, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa, inset: their optical images of control group (lift) and experimental group (right) under daylight and UV lamp; (B) Fluorescence spectra of CdSe QDs (biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa) of control group and some experimental groups; (C) Bright field images (top) and fluorescence images (bottom) of C. pyrenoidosa cells in the absence of Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 (a), Na2SeO3 (b) and Cd(NO3)2 (c) respectively, and in the presence of Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 (d); (D) FT-IR spectrum of CdSe QDs biosynthesize by C. pyrenoidosa.

(A) TEM images of C. pyrenoidosa co-incubated without Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 (control group); (B) TEM images of C. pyrenoidosa co-incubated with Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 for 12 days (experimental group); (C) TEM images of intracellular CdSe QDs; (D) HRTEM image of intracellular CdSe QDs, inset: particle size distribution histogram of QDs.

(A) TEM images of S. obliquus co-incubated without Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 (control group); (B) TEM images of S. obliquus co-incubated with Na2SeO3 and Cd(NO3)2 for 12 days (experimental group); (C) TEM images of intracellular CdSe QDs; (D) HRTEM image of intracellular CdSe QDs, inset: particle size distribution histogram of QDs.

(A) EDX spectrum of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa, inset: SAED image; (B) EDX spectrum of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by S. obliquus, inset: SAED image; (C) XRD patterns of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus.

(A) The GSH contents of C. pyrenoidosa in different incubation time; (B) The GSH contents of S. obliquus in different incubation time; (C) The mechanism diagram for the biosynthesis of CdSe QDs by the S. obliquus cell.

(A) Effects of different CdSe QDs concentrations on the FL intensities and fluorescence quenching rates upon addition of 0.20 mg mL−1 imatinib (left to right: 0.50, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 3.01 and 3.51 mg mL−1); (B) Effects of pH values on the FL intensities upon addition of 0.20 mg mL−1 imatinib; (C) Effects of reaction time on the FL intensities (3.01 mg mL−1 CdSe QDs upon addition of 0.20 mg mL−1 imatinib, and PBS buffer of pH 3.0); (D) Fluorescence spectra of CdSe QDs in the presence of various imatinib concentrations (from a to j: 0.00, 10.43, 20.87, 41.73, 62.60, 83.46, 104.33, 125.19, 208.65 and 417.30 μg/mL) in a pH 3.0 PBS system, inset: linear range of the plot of F0/F against imatinib concentrations.
Test for the interference of coexisting substances.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly intracellular microalgae synthesis of fluorescent CdSe QDs as a sensitive nanoprobe for determination of imatinib

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing, 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, the bio-immobilization methods displayed great potential for the removal and recycle of highly toxic metals depending on their gentle and orderly regulated cellular environment. Herein, we have presented a facile and eco-friendly strategy for bio-immobilizing cadmium (Cd) ions and to harvest CdSe quantum dots (QDs) using microalgae cells as bio-reactors. The production process has good environmental benefits for recycling hazardous Cd metal. The biosynthesized CdSe QDs exhibited superior optical properties, good water-solubility, high crystallinity structure, and were monodispersed sphere nanoparticles with approximate range of 5–6 nm in size, clearly comfirmed by the inversed fluorescent microscope, Ultraviolet-visible, photoluminescence, transmission electron microscopy analyses and so on. Owning to their fluorescence quenching effect, the as-obtained CdSe QDs could be deemed as an effective fluorencent nanoprobe for imatinib detection. The linear range for imatinib was calculated to be from 10.43 μg mL−1 to 417.30 μg mL−1 with a low detection limit of 0.014 μg mL−1. More strikingly, the possible formation mechanism of CdSe QDs in algal cells was preliminarily speculated that could be related with the intracellular GSH, which could provide new insights for the possibility for environmental transformation of poisonous metals to fluorescent QDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) with unique optical and electronic properties have wide applications in sensors [1,2], diagnosis [3], biological imaging [4,5], catalysis [6], and electro-optic devices [7]. These applications, particularly applied for biosensing and drug delivery, require proper design and synthesis of QDs [8]. Conventional strategies, mainly containing both physical and chemical methods, have been comfirmed to be energy inefficiency and environmental hazards because of being involved toxic chemicals, highly corrosive reagents, harsh and cumbersome reaction conditions, and easy production of harmful substances. Therefore, it is still of great value to explore a simple, nontoxic, and eco-friendly biological route for the fabrication of fluorescent QDs.

Currently, natural and efficient biosystem as a platform to synthesize the nanoparticles (NPs) has sparked tremendous comcern due to its clean and sustainable feature, which could be promising alternatives for overcoming above restrictions [9]. The biosynthesis of NPs is mainly through a rational coupling of intrinsic biochemical pathways that occur in a mild and highly organized cellular environment. Particularly, the NPs synthesized by living organisms are commonly capped with proteins or peptides, resulting in numerous intriguing properties encompassing stable optical properties, good water-solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [10,11]. Up to now, many environmental organisms and model microorganisms, including bacteria [12], algae [13], fungi [14] and earthworm [15], have been utilized successfully for the biosynthesis of NPs.

The algae-mediated synthetic approachs of NPs have attracted remarkable interests due to low cost, environmental protection, and outstanding environmental adaptability. In a comparion to other bio-reactors, the algae, belonging to eukaryotic oxygenic photoautotrophs, can be easier to accumulate various heavy metals. The algae-mediated biosynthetic methods of NPs are based on either intracellular reduction by living cells or extracellular reduction by the crude extracts of microalgae biomass [16]. El-Rafie et al. [17] utilized polysaccharides extracted from four marine macro-algae to synthesize Ag NPs with particle size of 20 nm, which displayed good antimicrobial activity aganist Escherichia coli or Staphylococcus aureus. Similarly, Au NPs had been prepared by the extracted protein from blue green alga, and exihibited wally antibacterial activity against Gram positive bacteria [18]. Except for the crude extracts of alga, Au NPs could be also fabricated by living chlorella [19] or diatomaceous cells [20], which effectively simplified synthetic procedures and provided exciting evidences for the formation or store of NPs in living algal cells. Even more exciting was the finding that some other nanomaterials like ZnO and Fe NPs were synthesized successfully by the algal extracts [21,22]. However, the literatures mainly focused on the synthesis of NPs using the algal extracts to date, but only very few reports on the production of fluorescent QDs by living algal cells.

Herein, we present a facile and totally green strategy for the bio-immobilization of Cd ions and to harvest CdSe QDs in aqueous media using Chlorella pyrenoidosa (C. pyrenoidosa) and Scenedesmus obliquus (S. Obliquus) as bio-reactors. For all we know, this is the first report on the biosynthesis of fluorescent CdSe QDs by microalgae cells. More strinkingly, the biosynthesized CdSe QDs exhibited superior performances when deemed as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of imatinib. This work not only can offer further insights into the development of biosynthesis approach that produces fluorescent QDs, but also broaden the applications of biosynthesized nanosensor.

The microalgae cells (C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus) were obtained from Institute of Hydrobiology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (China). Chromium chloride (CdCl2) and cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (USA). Sodium selenite (Na2SeO3) was gained by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Selemium powder was purchased from Shanghai Meixing Chemical Co., Ltd (China). Imatinib (4-[(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)methyl]-N-(4-methyl-3-{[4-(pyridin-3-yl)pyrimidin-2-yl]amino}phenyl)benzamide) was supported by Guangzhou Yang Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (China). All other chemicals were of analytical grade or the best grade commercially available. Ultrapure water or deionized water was used throughout the experiments.

The photographs of microalgae cells were recorded by a DP72 inversed fluorescent microscope (Olympus, Japan). Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy was performed on a UV-1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). All photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured with a RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained using a IRAffinity-1S spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were carried out using a FEI Tecnai G2 TEM operation (FEI, USA) at an accelerating voltage of 200 Kv equipped with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) datum were collected on an ARL X'TRA diffractometer (Thermo, USA) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) with a tube voltage of 40 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosynthesis of CdSe QDs by microalgae cells
ce:section.ce:para: The microalgae cells (C. pyrenoidosa) were maintained in the sterile Blue-Green medium at 25 °C illumination incubator with a light intensity of 4000 lx (12/12 h light/dark cycle). Prior to CdSe QDs biosynthesis, C. pyrenoidosa were seeded and cultured in 500 mL Erlenmeyer flask to reach an initial density of 1 × 10^6 cells/mL. As illustrated in Fig. S1 and S2, firstly, the exponentially growing algae cells were harvested from the medium by centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 15 min, and suspended in 20 mL sterile deionized water. Subsequently, 200 μL of 0.1 mol/L Na2SeO3 solution was introduced into algae cells. After seleniumized for 12 h, 200 μL of 0.1 mol/L Cd (NO3)2 solution was added and co-incubated for 12 days to biosynthesize the CdSe QDs. During the co-incubation process, the microalgae suspension was observed to change from green to yellow, and no further color variation occurred after 12 days, suggesting the successful formation of CdSe QDs that exhibited bright yellow-orange luminescence under UV lamp. Finally, the CdSe QDs were isolated from algae cells burst by ultrasonic vibration (JY92-II, Scientz Biotechnology Co., Ltd, China). Moreover, growth in sterile deionized water without selenite or cadmium sources was served as control groups. Besides, the synthetic process of S. obliquus was similar to the method mentioned above.

For measurement of the intracellular reduced glutathione (GSH), microalgal cells (C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus) were centrifuged and then washed thrice with deionized water. The harvested cell pellets were decomposited under an ice-cold condition by a Mini-Bead Beater for 30 min. The resulting supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 4500 rpm, and determined the total reduced GSH contents according to the microtiter-based method reported previously [23].

For determination of imatinib in water samples, the assay solution of CdSe QDs (3.01 mg mL−1) was obtained by diluting stock solution (approximate 55.64 mg mL−1) with phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 3.0). Subsequently, 10 μL various concentrations of imatinib were added into 2 mL assay solution and kept at a stable room temperature for 2 min to achieve the completion of the reaction. Finally, the fluorescence (FL) intensity (F, CdSe QDs solution with adding erlotinib) was recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at 480 nm. Simultaneously the FL intensity of blank test (F0, CdSe QDs solution without adding erlotinib) was measured under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence photographs of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus with different incubation stages were recorded to monitor the growth of QDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , algal cells of control group without treatment of selenite and cadmium sources always appeared dark-red under exciting light, which could be attributed to the chlorophyll of living algal cells that displayed dark-red fluorescence. However, the images of algal cells treated with both cadmium and selenium sources showed that the yellow-green fluorescence was initially observed nearby the cell membrane of algal cells when co-incubated for 4 days, and reached the strongest at 12th day, indicating that the cadmium and selenium were absorbed into the cells and successfully generated CdSe QDs by intracellular reducing biomolecules, which was coincided with the CdSe QDs biosynthesized by the microbes [24]. In addition, it was obviously observed that the cells of control group always maintained unbroken cell morphology during the incubation, while the experimental group gradually lost the chlorophyll after adding cadmium source, indicating that the Cd ions badly damage the algal cells.

The optical properties of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by two kinds of microalgae cells (C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus) were researched by the typical UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 A (curve a), the CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa have two specific absorption peaks centered at 300 and 372 nm, which are ascribed to the protein absorption and first quantum confinement absorption of CdSe QDs, respectively [24]. Similarly, the CdSe QDs biofabricated by S. obliquus also have an obvious absorption shoulder at around 302 nm and a first quantum confinement absorption band (approximately 365 nm) (Fig. S3A, curve a). The CdSe QD biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus have similar fluorescence spectra that display strong emission peaks at 470 and 480 nm respectively (Fig. 2A, curve b and Fig. S3A, curve b), corresponding to the fabrication of CdSe QDs with a well-defined first excitonic peak [25]. The isolated CdSe QDs of experimental group are bright yellow-orange under UV lamp (365 nm, center), but control group only dark-red (inset in Fig. 2A and Fig. S3A). Furthermore, only algal cells grown in the presence of both cadmium and selenite sources for 12 days possess strong fluorescence. Other combinations of ingredients lacking cadmium or selenite sources display no fluorescence, indicating that the formation and photoluminescence of CdSe QDs only occurs in certain culture conditions containing cadmium, selenite and algae cells (Fig. 2B and C; Fig. S3B and S3C).

Depending on the previous literatures, protein molecules have strong ability to bind metal that can form an extraordinary layer covering the metal NPs to prevent agglomeration, enhance the stability in the medium, and even reduce biotoxicity [26,27]. In order to confirm this view, the surface functional groups present on the biosynthesized CdSe QDs were determined by the FT-IR spectra. As indicated in Fig. 2D, the spectrum of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by C. pyrenoidosa shows the presence of three bands at 1654, 1540 and 3304 cm−1, corresponding to amide I (1600–1690 cm−1), amide II (1480–1575 cm−1) bands (protein) and OH (hydroxyl) functional groups respectively, present in protein molecules, which well agreed with relative reference [28]. The absorption peaks at around 2920, 2856 and 1058 cm−1 can be easily identified that attributed to the stretching and bending modes of CH bond [29]. Overall, the substances wrapped CdSe QDs are identified as protein molecules, which have been considered as an effective route to enhance the stabilization and reduce the biotoxicity of nanomaterials. The same results were also obtained from the CdSe QDs biosynthesized by S. obliquus (Fig. S3D)

Meanwhile, TEM and XRD techniques were utilized to analyze the size and crystallinity of the biosynthesized CdSe QDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, the C. pyrenoidosa cells of control group present normal elliptical morphology and distinct boundary between the cell wall and interior, while experimental group are poor morphology but have many small spots around the cells that should be the biosynthesized CdSe QDs. Due to the biotoxicity of heavy metal, the cell wall can be damaged or burst open to make intracellular CdSe QDs released, so that the plentiful CdSe QDs are accumulated either in the cell cytoplasm or in the extracellular space. As indicated in Fig. 3C and D, the isolated CdSe QDs are well monodispersed and uniform spherical morphology with average size of 4.15 ± 0.05 nm (n = 134), which are smaller than that of previous reports [30]. And the analogous results were observed from the CdSe QDs biosynthesized by S. obliquus (Fig. 4 A–D). The representative image of an individual nanoparticle displays high crystallinity structure (Fig. 3D and Fig. 4D). Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 5 C, XRD analyses of the isolated nanoparticles illustrate that the patterns of nanocrystal have two relatively strong peaks at ∼23.44° and ∼28.74°, which can correspond to the (100) and (101) planes of the face-centered cubic structure of CdSe QDs, respectively [31]. The XRD patterns are also consistent with the lattice planes observed in previous HRTEM images. The EDX analyses are depicted in Fig. 5A and B, clearly revealing that our nanoparticles contain two elemental components of Cd and Se. Diffuse ring pattern acquired from SAED confirms that the CdSe QDs have some crystal lattices (inset in Fig. 5A and B), which are in agreement with the HRTEM results. In general, all these findings demonstrate the uniform size and good crystallinity of biosynthesized CdSe QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimum conditions for CdSe QDs biosynthesis by C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus
ce:section.ce:para: The schematic diagram for the biosynthesis of CdSe QDs by algae cells was illustrated in Fig. S1. Many critical parameters were investigated to access their influences in CdSe QDs production, including the types of selenium or cadmium source, concentration of selenite or cadmium, and exposure duration. As showed in Fig. S4A–E, the optimum conditions were eventually determined: C. pyrenoidosa suspended in sterile deionized water were exposed in 0.75 mmol/L of Na2SeO3 solution for 6 h, and then added Cd(NO3)2 with final concentration of 1.0 mmol/L for incubating 12 days.

Subsequently, the suitable condition for the biosynthesis of S. obliquus was also investigated with reference to that of C. pyrenoidosa, and the similar results were obtained (Fig. S5A–E). Briefly, different types of selenium or cadmium source were utilized to biosynthesize CdSe QDs, while their FL intensities varied greatly, indicating that they played crucial roles in the intracellular reaction direction of QDs biosynthesis (Fig. S5A). Besides, the same trends were shown in the concentration effects, which the FL intensities increased with the increase of Na2SeO3 or Cd(NO3)2 concentrations at first and reaches a maximum and then dropped gradually (Fig. S5B and S5C). It was speculated that the low synthetic productivity could be because of insufficient precursor, while superfluous selenite or Cd2+ had direct toxic effects on algal cells, which can greatly reduce cell vitality and enzymatic activity about the biosynthesis of QDs. Moreover, the low production efficiency may also be attributed to the inappropriate feed ratios of cadmium and selenium under high concentration conditions. Remarkably, the exposure time of Na2SeO3 play a vital role on the biosynthesis of CdSe QDs (Fig. S5D and S5E). According to the literature [24], the microbes have two metabolic pathways during the exposure of selenite containing the formation of organic selenium and reduction to elemental selenium. However, organic selenium can be utilized to biosynthesize the QDs by microbes and other organisms. Short exposure time of Na2SeO3 may result in the high valence of inorganic selenium will not be converted to organic selenium completely. On the other hand, excessive exposure time may cause higher expression of detoxification induced by organic selenium in algal cells, leading to organic selenium convert to elemental selenium and lost the ability to combine with Cd2+. These results were in conformity with the relevant reference which has reported the generation and quantitative change of organic selenium. Therefore, the optimum conditions for the CdSe QDs biosynthesied by S. obliquus were eventually set as: the cells suspended in sterile deionized water were exposed in 1.0 mmol/L of Na2SeO3 solution for 12 h, and then added Cd(NO3)2 with final concentration of 1.0 mmol/L for incubating 12 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of CdSe QDs formation by microalgae cells
ce:section.ce:para: Exploring the formation mechanism of intracellular NPs will contribute to further engineering microorganism toward nanomaterial biosynthesis. However, the formation mechanism of QDs by microalgae has not been clarified. According to the previous literatures, during the progress of algae-mediated biosynthesis of metal NPs, crucial parameters are mainly included the light and reducing biomolecules containing polysaccharides, enzymes, NADPH and NADPH depended reductase, which could accelerate the reduction of metallic ions in the cell membrane and cytoplasm via energy-generating reactions within the photosynthetic electron transport system (EST) [32,33]. The possible mechanisms of CdSe QDs biosynthesized by yeast cells [34,35] have been clearly summarized as the following steps: firistly, Na2SeO3 added into the yeast cell suspension immediately reacted with GSH to form GSSeSG. Subsequently, reduction of GSSeSG catalyzed by GSH-related enzymes (thioredoxin reductase and glutathione reductase) resulted in low-valenced organ selenium containing Se-Met and Se-Cys, which were crucial selenium precursors for the QDs formation. Finally, Cd(SG)2, originated from adding Cd ions at proper time, reacted with Se-Cys or Se-Met to produce the CdSe QDs.

In order to investigate possible mechanism of the algae-mediated biosynthesis of CdSe QDs, we mainly monitored the intracellular GSH content during the QDs formation progress. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A and 6B, the content of GSH in algae cells (C. pyrenoidosa and S. obliquus) gradually increased as the increase of incubating duration upon addition of Na2SeO3, while sharply decreased after added Cd (NO3)2 at 4th day and reached the lowest value at 12th day. These results confirmed that the selenite, transferred into algal cells, could be converted to organic selenium that was benefited to the cell growth, due to the increase of GSH secreted by algal cells under the exposure of selenite. However, once Cd(NO3)2 was added into algal cells, the generated GSH were constantly consumed to the production of CdSe QDs, and achieved the lowest value at 12th day, which was in agreement with fluorescence results above mentioned. Based on these presented results, we speculated that the Na2SeO3, internalized into algal cells, primarily reacted with the reducing biomolecules to generate selenium precursors within the EST system. Subsequently, selenium precursors were combined with Cd(SG)2 under the catalysis of relative reductase to form the CdSe QDs with protective protein. Finally, the as-protected QDs were accumulation in algal cytoplasm or released into culture media (Mechanism diagram was shown in Fig. 6C). As such, the fabrication processes of CdSe QDs by microalgae cells make good environmental benefit and low energy consumption for effective removal and recycle of hazardous Cd metal. This work can provide new insights for the possibility for environmental transformation of highly toxic metals to fluorescent QDs by microalgae cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Condition optimization of CdSe QDs-based nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The superior optical properties of biosynthesized CdSe QDs provide the opportunity for the sensing applications. When the CdSe QDs were performed in the presence of imatinib, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon was obviously observed (Fig. S6). To make the nanoprobe with a relatively wide linear range and high sensitivity, some parameters of the system were optimized containing the dosage of CdSe QDs, medium pH, and reaction temperature and time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of the dosage of CdSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The dosage of CdSe QDs not only affects the initial fluorescence intensity (F0), but also influences the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0). Moreover, high initial FL intensity and (F0-F)/F0 could provide a wide linear range and high sensitivity for fluorescence assay respectively. Considering these factors, as presented in Fig. 7 A, 100 μg mL−1 of CdSe QDs solution was chosen for the determination of imatinib in following study.

The pH of the medium greatly influences the (F0-F)/F0 of fluorescent QDs [36]. As shown in Fig. 7B, the (F0-F)/F0 of the nanoprobe for imatinib detection increased with varying the solution pH values from 1.0 to 3.0 whereas decreased gradually in the range of 3.0 and 11.0, verifying that the fluorescence properties of the detection system intensively rely on the pH value of the medium. Based on higher fluorescence quenching efficiency, pH 3.0 was taken as the optimized buffer condition for imatinib detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of reaction temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: The reaction temperature and time also could affect the fluorescence quenching process. As shown in Fig. S7, the (F0-F)/F0 of the system slightly changed with the variation of ambient temperature. In addition, as can be observed in Fig. 7C, the fluorescence response upon addition of imatinib became stable after 2 min, indicating the fluorescence quenching reaction achieved the balance. Consequently, the FL intensity was recorded under a stable ambient temperature after adding imatinib into the detection system for the quenching process of 2 min.

Under above optimum conditions, analytical performances of this nanoprobe were investigated in detail. As illustrated in Fig. 7D, the FL intensities of CdSe QDs at 460 nm are remarlably sensitive to imatinib, and gradually decrease with increasing imatinib concentration. There is a good linear correlation between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and concentration of imatinib (inset in Fig. 7) as the following equation:
F0/F = 0.0052C + 0.9946, R2 = 0.9984

The selectivity of this sensing system was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence signal changes of CdSe QDs in the presence of imatinib and various metal ions. As depicted in Table 1 , except for Cu2+, these common metal ions have no obvious interference (fluorescence quenching or enhancement) compared to imatinib. These results suggested that the proposed nanoprobe could be highly selective and provide credible results for imatinib detection even under high concentration of interfering substances.

Moreover, accuracy is a vital parameter to evaluate the analytical method [38,39]. In order to further assess its practicality, we performed the recovery tests that determined imatinib in water samples through the standard addition method. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery results at different concentrations of sample solution are in the range of 99%–101% that satisfied analytical requirements, indicating that the nanoprobe could restrict interference effects and be enough accurate for assay of real sampes. Overall, the biosynthesized CdSe QDs, utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe, displayed excellent chemical performances for matinib sensing.

In summary, a facile and eco-friendly strategy for bio-immobilizing cadmium ions and to biosynthesize nano-sized fluorescent CdSe QDs using microalgae cells (C. pyrenoidosa and S. Obliquus) as bio-reactors was presented. The production processes make good environmental benefit and low energy consumption for effective removal and recycle of harmful cadmium metal. The biosynthesized CdSe QDs display superior fluorescence properties, good crystallinity, and monodispersed and uniform spherical morphology with approximate range of 4–5 nm in size. Furthermore, the CdSe QDs utilized as a fluorescent nanoprobe possess outstanding analytical performances for matinib sensing. This work not only can provide new insights for the possibility for environmental transformation of highly toxic metals to fluorescent QDs by microalgae cells, but also broaden promising applications of biosynthesized nanoprobes.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21507165) and the National College Students' Innovation Entrepreneurial Training Program (201710316194).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.02.169.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of nanoparticles: sunlight formation of gold nanodecahedra for ultra-sensitive lead-ion detection
A fluorescent sensing for glycoproteins based on the FRET between quantum dots and Au nanoparticles
Hydrothermal synthesis of GSH-TGA co-capped CdTe quantum dots and their application in labeling colorectal cancer cells

Ultrasmall near-infrared Ag2Se quantum dots with tunable fluorescence for in vivo imaging

Quantum dots for live cells, in vivo imaging, and diagnostics
Mechanism-oriented controllability of intracellular quantum dots formation: the role of glutathione metabolic pathway

biosynthesis of biocompatible CdSe quantum dots in living Escherichia coli cells

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhengwei Zhang is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. His research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
Jiao Chen is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research focuses on drug spectral analysis.
Qiulian Yang is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research focuses on drug spectral analysis.
Kai Lan is currently a junior undergraduate student of school of science, China Pharmaceutical University.
Zhengyu Yan is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
Jianqiu Chen is currently a pharmaceutical analysis researcher a at China Pharmaceutical University. His research focuses on synthesis of novel materials and application in drug analysis.
